result:
I want to talk about how the smell of rain on hot asphalt can yank entire years out of the ground by the roots. The French have a tidy little phrase for it—petrichor—but that word feels too clinical for a sensation that always arrives with a gasp. When the first drops hit and the gray pearl-coat on the street begins to sizzle, My body doesn’t just remember: it unzips time. Suddenly I’m eight, kneeling beside the crack in our driveway where ants became frantic archipelagos, the same driveway where I practiced the phrase “I don’t love you anymore” in case I ever had to tell a future ex-girlfriend something true. I was eight: I had no girlfriend, only a cheap two-band radio pressed to my ear because thunderstorms knocked Florida stations into Ohio, and a deejay with a velvet voice was promising that music could cross state lines without asking permission.

Dictionaries call that porous boundary between past and present “involuntary memory,” like it’s a legal term or an indictment. I prefer to think of it as weather-splicing, the way film editors cut two shots of clouds so the dissolve looks inevitable. Inside that splice, the adult who pays bills and programs the coffee maker for 4:41 a.m. ceases to exist for three humid seconds. There is only the child inventor of things that never got patented: a toothpaste-tube squeezer made from Tinkertoys, a bicycle that braked when humming the opening riff to “Back in Black.” 

We forget that time is porous until something forces the holes open. A song we haven’t heard since cassette culture loops through a pharmacy speaker, and there we are—sixteen, parking-lot fluorescent, too anxious to say yes to the girl offering cinnamon Tic-Tacs. Or a kitchen fills with the smell of cardamom, and overnight the current decade frays until we’re six again, staring up at a great-aunt who is teaching the concept of “pinch” versus “dash” while the dough rises like yeasty faith. 

I used to chalk these flashes up to nostalgia, a vanilla word nostalgia-hunters on social media turned into an insult, shorthand for “can’t move on.” But the longer I live, the more I suspect they aren’t backward glances so much as inaccessible futures. The child I was still has plausible futures attached to him, like paper lanterns you can’t release until the air inside is hotter than outside. Whenever I smell rain-boiled asphalt, those lanterns lift an inch and tease the adult sky. They say: We were real, and somewhere, still burning. 

This is why I collect trivial smells the way amateur herpetologists pocket smooth stones. Dolly Parton’s “Jolene” on vinyl has a smell—cardboard sleeve warm from the amplifier—different from the same song on 320-kbps MP3, which has no smell at all. A library book from 1988 smells like oat-bran cereal and wood pulp, but if it went through a flood it carries underwater nursery rhyme notes, mildew and stalagmite. I store them in the skull behind my nose, an organ scholars call the olfactory bulb, although bulb is a misnomer for what feels more like a plunging elevator. 

Years ago I interviewed an astronaut who told me the smell of space is “like arc-welding on an old fishing boat.” Think hot metal, but near something that once carried sunburned hope. He said when he re-entered Earth’s atmosphere and began to smell woodsmoke from continents below, he wept at how complicated home had become: a fractal of campfires and factories and bakerilla beans mixed into one breath. I wrote the line down—how could I not—yet the interview continued and the line stayed flat on the page, refusing to blossom into the actual ache he described. You can’t transmit someone else’s cathedral of scent; the best you can do is lean close and invite a reader to guess.

There’s a lizard in the desert called the thorny devil who collects water through microscopic grooves on its skin. Any drop that lands—dew, fog, the exhalation of a night-blooming cactus—races downhill toward the corners of its mouth. In place of sobs or arguments, the little creature uses architecture for thirst. Memory works similarly: tiny grooves shaped like playground swing-sets or the perfume aisle at Woolworth’s or hospital bleach. A droplet lands and is routed inward, delivered to the exact spot still cracked open. You can’t legislate which groove the drop will take; the body is both desert and storm cloud.

Last July I stood in line at a bakery where someone behind the counter was burning a clove cigarette just outside the side door. The law says you can’t smoke near food prep, but there are grey zones made of porous time. One lungful of clove and I was nineteen on an Indonesian ferry, clutching a postcard addressed to a girl whose name I’ve since misspelled in multiple address books. The ferry smelled of diesel and pandan, but the clove note threaded through like a violin string tuned too high. I never mailed the postcard. When the cashier asked me “baguette or sourdough,” I spun around expecting to see the sea and instead saw plastic flowers wired to the ceiling. For half a heartbeat I hated the adult who had let the letter stay unsent, until I realized it had arrived after all, if via detour.

Not all resurrections feel gentle. I once walked past a bus belching blue-gray exhaust and remembered my father trying to siphon gasoline during the 1979 oil crisis. He got some in his mouth, spat twice, laughed, ran the engine anyway. The memory hit while I was standing beside a completely different father and his daughter waiting for a pedestrian light. He bent to tie her shoe; I tasted gasoline again and panicked because I couldn’t warn him that someday memory itself might become a toxic mouthful. But how do you hand a stranger the postcard of your future grief?

The poet Elizabeth Bishop ended “One Art” with the terrifying line “—even losing you (the joking voice, a gesture I love)”—and the parenthetical phrase is the clove in the diesel air. Loss isn’t confined to cathedrals or cemeteries; it ambushes us in checkout lines, bus exhaust, cardamom steam. The only difference between a goodbye and a haunting is repetition. We practice goodbyes, rewinding them, smelling them, letting time rain until the asphalt releases its carried oceans.

I think that’s why children invent mechanical solutions to invisible problems. A toothpaste squeezer, a song-powered brake—they’re attempts to control the escape of something precious. They don’t yet know the physics: when you squeeze a tube, you’re also pressing memory outward, shaping a path for future grooves. It gets in the cracks like rainwater, hot and particular, ungoverned by statutes.  

The space-suited astronaut thought he had left the planet. But the planet, it turned out, was riding shotgun in every molecule of air once the hatch resealed. Likewise the child in the driveway never truly steps inside my house; he is always out there, kneeling, practicing brand-new heartbreaks he will not remember how to say aloud until the next cloudburst. When the first fat drop lands, we both look up and for a second neither of us knows which century owns the sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
